nativeRange.habitat: Still and sluggish freshwater — rice paddies, swamps, roadside ditches, lowland ponds, and slow streams, often with dense vegetation and brown-to-clear water

decorPreferences: dense planting with tall and floating plants
leaf litter and driftwood for tannins and cover
caves, pots, or tube hides for retreat
gentle sponge filtration — strong currents should be avoided
tight-fitting lid mandatory (labyrinth fish escape readily)

diet.notes: Micro-carnivore in the wild, feeding on small invertebrates, zooplankton, and insect larvae. In captivity, generally accepts quality micro-pellets and flake food once acclimated, but tends to thrive with regular offerings of small live or frozen foods such as bloodworms, daphnia, and brine shrimp. Small frequent meals are generally preferred over large single feedings.

goodWith: other Betta imbellis in well-planted, adequately sized tanks
avoidWith: Betta splendens (hybridizes readily — keep strictly separate)

summary: One of the most peaceful wild-form Betta species, native to Malaysian and Sumatran wetlands. Males display stunning iridescent blue-green scaling with a distinctive red crescent on the caudal fin. Intermediate care due to soft-acidic water needs and escape risk.
careNotes:
Betta imbellis is commonly considered one of the most peaceful Betta species — hence the epithet imbellis (Latin: "peaceful" or "unwarlike"). Unlike B. splendens, males of this species tend to display toward each other without escalating to lethal combat, and can sometimes be co-housed in sufficiently large, densely planted tanks. However, this is not a guarantee; individual males vary, and adequate cover and sight-line breaks are essential if multiple males are attempted.

A heater is needed — this is a tropical species. Soft, acidic water is strongly preferred; peat filtration, leaf litter (Indian almond leaves / catappa), and driftwood are all useful for achieving the blackwater conditions this species encounters in the wild. kH should be kept low to avoid pH buffering against the target acidic range.

This species hybridizes freely with Betta splendens. Wild-form specimens should never be housed alongside B. splendens, and captive lines of B. imbellis are sometimes contaminated with B. splendens genetics. When sourcing, wild-caught or reputable specialist-bred stock is preferred to preserve wild coloration and species integrity.

A tight-fitting lid is mandatory — labyrinth fish are strong jumpers, particularly during territorial displays or courtship pursuits. Surface access must remain unobstructed for atmospheric breathing.

Can be kept as a pair, or as a sorority/group of 4+ individuals in a well-planted tank of 75L+. The species has been extensively line-bred for fighting in parts of Thailand, so aggression levels can vary between lines. Wild-type coloration (dark body, iridescent blue-green scaling, red crescent caudal) is generally preferred by enthusiasts over fancy domesticated lines.

Female coloration is more subdued but still attractive. Both sexes are generally sold; unlike B. splendens, females are not typically culled from production.
breedingNotes: Bubble-nest builder with paternal care. Males construct a floating nest of mucus-coated bubbles at the surface, typically anchored under floating plants or a leaf. Courtship involves lateral displays and fin-spreading. Spawning is generally described as relatively easy by hobbyist accounts, with clutch sizes typically smaller than B. splendens. Egg incubation tends to take 24–48 hours; fry become free-swimming a few days later and require infusoria-grade first foods (vinegar eels, microworms, or commercial fry powder). Male guards the nest; the female should generally be removed or given ample cover post-spawning, though males of this species have been reported to be less aggressive post-spawn than B. splendens.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Primary source (Aquarium Co-Op returned 404 for this species; SeriouslyFish swapped in as primary). Provided size (50–60 mm), temperature (20–28°C), pH (5.0–7.5), hardness (18–179 ppm / approx 1–10 dGH), tank footprint (45×30 cm / ~41 L minimum for pair), native range, habitat, diet, breeding, and temperament.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed family (Osphronemidae), order (Anabantiformes), max size (6.0 cm SL), temperature (24–28°C), pH (6.0–7.0), hardness (5–19 dH), native range (Thailand, Malaysia, Singapore, Indonesia), IUCN Least Concern, bubble-nest reproduction, carnivore diet.

additional.name: Japanese Fighting Fish
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (2–2.5 in / ~5–6.4 cm), temperature (70–82°F / 21–28°C), lifespan (~4 years), minimum tank (10 gal / 38L single; 30 gal / 114L for multiples), diet, temperament, and grouping notes. Water hardness not specified. Fishkeeping World and Practical Fishkeeping returned 404/403.

fish.breedingNotes: Bubble-nest builder; paternal care; male guards nest. Female should be removed or given refuge post-spawn. Fry require infusoria-grade first foods. Generally regarded as one of the easier wild Betta species to breed in captivity.
